name: Quadrantids
description: Brief but intense shower with a sharp peak lasting only a few hours. Best viewed from the Northern Hemisphere.

name: Lyrids
description: One of the oldest known meteor showers, observed for over 2,700 years. Produces bright, fast meteors with occasional fireballs.

name: Eta Aquariids
description: Fast meteors produced by debris from Halley's Comet. Best viewed from the Southern Hemisphere where the radiant rises higher.

name: Delta Aquariids
description: A steady, moderate shower best seen from southern latitudes. Often overlaps with the early Perseids.

name: Perseids
description: The most popular meteor shower of the year, known for bright meteors and frequent fireballs during warm summer nights.

name: Draconids
description: Slow-moving meteors best viewed in the early evening. Occasionally produces dramatic outbursts of hundreds per hour.

name: Orionids
description: Fast, bright meteors from Halley's Comet debris. Known for producing persistent trains visible for several seconds.

name: Taurids (South)
description: A long-duration shower with slow, bright meteors. Known for producing spectacular fireballs despite low hourly rates.

name: Taurids (North)
description: Complements the Southern Taurids with similarly slow, bright fireballs. Together they create weeks of Taurid activity.

name: Leonids
description: The fastest bright meteors of any annual shower. Produces meteor storms roughly every 33 years with thousands per hour.

name: Geminids
description: The strongest and most reliable meteor shower of the year. Produces bright, multi-colored meteors at high rates.

name: Ursids
description: A modest shower near the winter solstice. The circumpolar radiant means it is visible all night from northern latitudes.
